title: The Geography of Financial Misconduct
abstract: Financial misconduct (FM) rates differ widely between major U.S. cities, up to a factor of 3. Although spatial differences in enforcement and firm characteristics do not account for these patterns, city-level norms appear to be very important. For example, FM rates are strongly related to other unethical behavior, involving politicians, doctors, and (potentially unfaithful) spouses, in the city.

article.introduction:
Traditional models of crime begin with Becker (1968), who frames the choice to engage in misbehavior like any other economic decision involving cost-benefit tradeoffs. Though somewhat successful when taken to the data, the theory fails to account for the enormous variation in crime rates observed across both time and space. Indeed, as Glaeser, Sacerdote, and Scheinkman (1996) argue, regional variation in demographics, enforcement, and other observables is simply not large enough to explain why, for example, two seemingly identical neighborhoods in the same city have drastically different crime rates.1Their conclusion is that social factors—culture—play an important role in a person's decision to break rules, or to respect them.
After providing a stylized model (SectionI) and description of the data (SectionII), we begin our empirical analysis with a measurement exercise in SectionIII. Here, we measure regional differences in FM, provide a feel for the magnitudes, and ask whether FM events cluster geographically more than would be predicted by chance. In other words, although simple averages indicate that FM is three times more common in some places (e.g., Miami, Dallas) than others (e.g., Seattle, Minneapolis), are these differences statistically significant?
We follow the empirical approach of Ellison and Swanson (2016), who use negative binomial regressions to examine whether the incidence of exceptionally high scores on a difficult high school mathematics exam cluster at the school level. The variable of interest in our study, FM, is also a rare event, and like the number of high test scores, the number of firms committing financial misconduct takes discrete values.
In the negative binomial regression we estimate, the number of FM events occurring in each city over our four-decade sample period is regressed on the population at risk (total number of firm-years). We estimate a highly significant (p<0.001) excess variance parameter, which indicates that FM clusters more in certain cities than we would expect by chance. The same picture emerges if we expand the unit of analysis to the city-year level, resulting in 20 cities × 40 years = 800 city-year observations, and estimate city fixed effects. In Poisson regressions, city fixed effects are highly significant (p<0.001) determinants of FM incidence.3
With these basic patterns established, in the remainder of the paper we attempt to understand the factor(s) underlying this geographic heterogeneity in FM. We consider three possibilities. First, the risk of detection or enforcement may differ across cities, in which case differences inobservedmisconduct may arise even if actual FM rates are identical. Second, the potential benefit of “cooking the books” may vary across cities, for example, with regional differences in financial distress or incentive-based compensation. Finally, social attitudes toward right and wrong may differ across cities: a given behavior may be frowned upon in Minneapolis, questionable in Phoenix, but acceptable in Houston.
While we cannot definitively rule out the first two explanations, city-level social norms appear to be a key factor determining the prevalence of FM. In SectionIV, we assemble city-level data on a wide variety of activities intended to capture a city's prevailing ethical norms: (1) political corruption, (2) fraud by financial advisors, (3) backdating of executive stock options, (4) spousal infidelity, and (5) financial relationships between doctors and drug companies. Importantly, these behaviors span the range from being explicitly illegal (#1 and most cases of #2) to violating social contracts and/or generalized expectations for proper behavior (cases #3 to #5); in the latter three cases, formal enforcement plays little to no role.
In cross-sectional analyses we find that the prevalence of these questionable behaviors is strongly related to rates of FM committed by local executives. Despite the low statistical power of regressions estimated using only 20 observations (one for each city), the individually estimated coefficients on the incidence of each activity are all positive, with four significant at the 5% level, and the fifth (financial advisor fraud) exceeding the 10% threshold. When we combine these activities into a single city-level index based on their average ranks, thet-statistic is over four. The strength of this relation (ρ = 0.60) is readily apparent in Figure2, which plots each city's average FM rate against its ranking of other misbehaviors.
Remarkably, this index provides a near-completeaccount of the (significant) cross-city variation in FM. When we omit the misbehavior index as a covariate, the random effect parameter in a negative binomial regression of FM has ap-value of less than 0.001, indicating that FM clusters too frequently in some cities to be explained by chance. However, when we include the misbehavior index, thep-value increases to 0.5, implying that any residual heterogeneity across cities can be attributed to sampling variation.
While we interpret these results as strong evidence that city-level culture affects a wide range of misbehaviors, including financial misconduct, our analysis does not identify the source(s) of these norms. Following Manski's (1993) canonical taxonomy, city-level norms could arise from exogenous (e.g., genetic) differences between people in certain areas,4correlated/environmental influences (e.g., church or educational institutions), or endogenous peer effects. Any of these, or their combination, can contribute to city-wide misbehavior.
Following prior work, we include variables that have been linked with potential financial misconduct such as religious attendance, as well as various controls for demographic characteristics (e.g., age, population growth), economic conditions, and education. While these variables appear to matter somewhat, (1) they collectively explain less of the variation in FM than the misbehavior index alone, (2) unlike the misbehavior index, they do not eliminate excess clustering across cities, and (3) their inclusion has almost no effect on the coefficient of the misbehavior index. Thus, while norms appear to be important for understanding misbehavior, pinpointing their determinants is more challenging.
SectionVconsiders whether controlling for differential enforcement or firm characteristics changes these conclusions. To address the role of enforcement, we use two proxies identified by prior research as related to enforcement incentives: firm size (Dyck, Morse, and Zingales (2014)) and physical proximity to a U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) office (Kedia and Rajgopal (2011)). The results remain virtually unchanged even among large firms, and are similarly unaffected by controlling for SEC proximity. To assess potential regional heterogeneity in firm attributes, we augment the negative binomial model to account for city-level averages of potential FM determinants such as executive compensation and leverage. Although we find that, for example, firms with a high average debt ratio have a higher incidence of corporate misconduct, the collective explanatory power of firm-level characteristics is small and, importantly, has virtually no effect on the city-level misbehavior index.
Finally, while in most of the paper we focus on the cross-section, we conduct a brief analysis of time-series patterns in FM within cities. When we consider observations at the firm-year level, we find that firms are more likely to commit FM when their industry (nonlocal) peers do so, as well as when the FM rate for local (nonindustry) peers is higher than average. The latter effect suggests that, in addition to average cross-sectional differences, cities seem to experience ebbs and flows in the propensity of local firms committing misconduct. Unfortunately, most of the city-level misbehaviors in our index (e.g., spousal infidelity) are not available over long time series, which limits inference on the dynamics of local norms, including the rate at which they spread via social interactions.
Our first finding—measuring regional differences in FM—contributes to studies in which geography features such as Kedia, Koh, and Rajgopal's (2015) study of contagion in misreporting between industry and locational peers, or Kedia and Rajgopal's (2011) exploration of SEC office proximity as a deterrent. The second contribution—the interpretation that culture and/or ethics are the primary sources of these regional differences—adds to the literature that links questionable accounting to proxies for social norms such as religion (e.g., Grullon, Kanatas, and Weston (2009), Dyreng, Mayew, and Williams (2012), McGuire, Omer, and Sharp (2012)) or ethnicity (Liu (2016)). In SectionIV.D, we discuss various ways social norms can be measured, paying special attention to the limitations of our preferred approach.
From a forensic perspective, our findings contribute to studies that predict financial misconduct and/or misreporting at the firm level. Prior work identifies a number of relevant factors, which include firm performance (Harris and Bromiley (2007)), manager or director career concerns (Fich and Shivdasani (2007)), compensation arrangements (Burns and Kedia (2006), Erickson, Hanlon, and Maydew (2006), Efendi, Srivastava, and Swanson (2007)), institutional monitoring, and the strength of enforcement (Kedia and Rajgopal (2011)).5Our study suggests that social norms, identified by observing the behavior of a firm's local peers (and not just the behavior of other corporate executives), are a first-order determinant of corporate misbehavior, and as such, should be of interest to regulators as well as academics.
Finally, our study relates to the literature on urban agglomeration. Beginning with Marshall (1890), economists have sought to understand the reasons behind spatial clustering of firms and individuals. Recent work deemphasizes geographical features (e.g., river access) and shifts focus to “people-based” externalities such as knowledge spillovers or pooling of labor markets that improve firm-worker matches.6While on net the existence of cities suggests that the benefits of agglomeration tend to outweigh the costs, our results suggest that not all externalities are positive. For just as proximity facilitates the spread of disease, the spillover of ideas and social norms can permit the diffusion of both prosocial and antisocial behaviors.
article.I. A Simple Model of Financial Misconduc:
The right-hand side of1is the expected cost of committing FM, which is equal to the rate of detection,X, times the sum of two costs. The first cost,cenf, is the penalty—both direct and indirect—that accrues to the manager in the event that FM is detected and enforced. Direct penalties include prison sentences, fines, or the disgorgement of personal gains. Indirect penalties include stock price declines, negative publicity, or reduced labor market opportunities for managers involved in fraud.7
The second cost,csoc, refers to social and/or cultural factors that may act as an additional impediment to FM. For example, executives exposed for FM may be more or less stigmatized in some regions than others. Cities with high levels of social intolerance for FM would have high values ofcsoc—thereby acting as a deterrent—whereas lower values would correspond to cities with less social penalty for misbehavior.
article.II. Data:
Our data set includes firms headquartered within or near any of the 20 largest metropolitan areas in the United States. The sample covers the years 1970 to 2010, the period for which data on FM (described next) are available. The location variable that we use is Compustat's ADDZIP, which gives the current zip code of each firm's headquarters or home office.9
The geographic unit that we employ is an “economic area” (EA), as defined by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. EAs are typically larger than metropolitan statistical areas and are designed to capture regions within which workers commute. Examples of EAs are Dallas-Arlington-Fort Worth, Washington, DC-Columbia-Baltimore, and San Francisco-Oakland-San Jose. We use the term “area” and “city” interchangeably throughout the paper. The first column of TableIshows that the average number of firms headquartered in each city ranges from almost 600 in New York to just under 30 in Indianapolis and Orlando.
Because formal enforcement is a selection criterion, the KKLM data are unlikely to capture minor offenses or accidental accounting irregularities.10Indeed, as KKLM and other studies (e.g., Dechow, Ge, and Schrand (2010)) show, most accounting restatements are not intentional and are associated with neither lawsuits nor enforcement actions. On the flip side, a high threshold for inclusion in the sample means that the actual incidence of FM is almost certainly underreported.
Using the KKLM set of financial misrepresentation events, for each company in our 20-city sample, we identify the firm-year observations in which a violation occurred.11We then aggregate and calculate averages for each city, which we summarize in TableI. Cities are ranked by average FM using the entire sample. At the low end are cities like Indianapolis (0.48%), Seattle (0.52%), and Minneapolis (0.64%). At the high end are Dallas (1.61%), St. Louis (1.64%), and Miami (1.66%), with rates about three times as high as those at the low end.
Panel A of Figure1shows that these patterns are strongly persistent. The 25% of cities with the lowest average FM rates (e.g., Seattle) are represented by a solid gray line, the 25% with the highest average FM rates by a solid black line, and the middle 50% with by a dotted line. The gray line is almost never above the black line, and in most years the same ordering using the overall average (black > dotted > gray) is preserved. Panel B shows that adjusting for industry-average FM rates, if anything, strengthens city-level persistence.
Data on federal convictions for corruption-related crimes by elected officials come from the “Report to Congress on the Activities and Operations of Public Integrity Section,” published by the DOJ. Following Glaeser and Saks (2006), we use the number of DOJ-prosecuted convictions for each DOJ district headquartered in the area.12Dividing this number by population results in a conviction rate per 1,000,000 inhabitants in the relevant region for each city. Returning to TableI, we see that, from 1970 to 2010, the typical city has a rate of DOJ-prosecuted convictions of approximately 3 per 1,000,000 inhabitants, with considerable regional variation. The highest corruption rates correspond to Washington, DC and Miami, while lower rates are observed in San Francisco, Minneapolis, and Seattle.
We obtain data on monetary transfers from 12 major pharmaceutical firms to prescribing physicians during 2010 from ProPublica's “Dollars for Docs” website (https://projects.propublica.org/docdollars). Payments are listed by firm, doctor, date, amount in dollars, and activity (if any), such as a speaking engagement, consulting arrangement, dinner, or gift. We merge these payment data with Medicare (Part D) prescriptions for each physician using ProPublica's Prescriber CheckUp (projects.propublica.org/checkup).13TableIshows the average sensitivities between drug company payments and prescriptions for each city, using the regression methodology employed by Engelberg, Parsons, and Tefft (2016) and described in more detail in the Appendix. Larger numbers indicate higher responsiveness by a city's physicians. For example, a transfer of $137 (the mean value) translates, on average, to only 5 additional prescriptions among Minneapolis physicians (lowest) but almost 40 in Miami (highest).
We estimate city-level rates of spousal infidelity using the website Ashley Madison, whose target clients are married people seeking an extramarital affair. In Fall 2015, a widely publicized hack resulted in the public release of name, address, and billing information of approximately 40 million Ashley Madison users.14In TableI, we report the intensity of Ashley Madison usage for each of the 20 cities in our sample. Because Ashley Madison is a web-based application, and because rates of Internet connectivity may differ regionally, we scale the number of accounts by the number of over-18 adults that the U.S. Census indicates have access to the Internet.15The city with the lowest Ashley Madison usage per capita is Indianapolis (0.56%), the median cities are Chicago (0.75%) and Cleveland (0.77%), and the highest city is Washington, DC (0.98%).
Data on option backdating are collected from three separate sources: (1) the list last updated by the WSJ's “Options Backdating Scorecard” website on September 4, 2007, (2) the “Yellow Card Trend Alert: Stock-Option Backdating Scandal” report last updated by Glass Lewis & Co. on March 16, 2007, and (3) the list last provided by Dow Jones News Service on September 6, 2007. This sample includes 279 unique firms identified as having potentially engaged in stock option grant backdating from 1996 through 2006.16TableIreports the percentage of firms in this sample in each of our 20 cities scaled by the total number of firms in each city. The incidence of backdating varies widely across cities, ranging from 0% (e.g., Orlando, Philadelphia) to over 13% (e.g., San Francisco, Houston).
Data on fraud committed by financial advisors (registered representatives with the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority) were graciously provided by the authors of Dimmock, Gerken, and Graham (2018). The original source for these data are historical Form U4 filings, which the authors obtained through a combination of a third-party data vendor (Meridian-IQ's Registered Representative Database) and Freedom of Information requests. The authors provided us with city-level average fraud rates, aggregated over their sample period (1999 through 2014), which correspond approximately to the last third of the KKLM data.
Misconduct is identified by client complaints that were eventually ruled in the client's favor (and not expunged) and that involved a significant monetary settlement (greater than $100,000). Common reasons for client complaints are unsuitable investments, excessive trading (churning), and misrepresentation. TableIshows how rates of financial advisor misconduct—the total number of client complaints from 1999 to 2014 scaled by population—differ across cities. Overall, the typical city has around 80 financial advisor complaints for every million residents, but these numbers vary by a factor of 6 (e.g., Miami has around 200 per million while Indianapolis has slightly more than 30 per million.)
Our analysis also incorporates a number of standard firm-level control variables, all of which are obtained from standard sources. Stock returns are from CRSP, firm fundamentals from Compustat, and CEO compensation variables from ExecuComp. Demographics data are from the U.S. Census Bureau; measures on religious adherence are obtained from the American Religion Data Archive (ARDA).17
article.III. City Effects in Financial Misconduc:
Our analysis begins with a set of measurement exercises that characterize potential differences in FM across the 20 largest U.S. metropolitan areas. SubsectionAstarts at the most basic level, taking a single observation for each city, and asking whether FM rates differ more than would be expected from sampling variation. We then expand the unit of analysis to the city-year pair in subsectionB, allowing us to estimate dynamic panel models with city fixed effects.
The summary statistics in TableIand Figure1provide a preliminary sense for how FM rates vary across cities. However, they do not tell us whether these differences are larger than we would expect from random variation, that is, whether FM tends to cluster regionally more than predicted by sampling variation. The analysis in this section addresses this question using a single observation for each city (N=20). The dependent variable is the number of firm-years for which FM occurs in a given city, with the population at risk (independent variable) being the total number of firm-years. For example, for firms based in Dallas, we observe 100 firm-years of FM, against 6,189 total firm-years in Dallas from 1970 to 2010.
In a standard Poisson regression, the dependent variable is assumed to be Poisson distributed with meaneXiβ, whereXiare covariates that influence the distribution. For example, larger cities with more firm-years at risk (e.g., New York) are naturally expected to have more FM occurrences than smaller ones (e.g., Indianapolis). However, we are mainly interested in theunexplainedvariation between cities. That is, do we observe more clustering in FM than sampling variation would predict under the null that the (conditional) Poisson distributions are identical across cities?
Accordingly, we permit the distribution to vary by city, so that the mean becomeseXiβui, whereuiis a multiplicative gamma-distributed term with a mean equal to one and variance equal to α. A value of 0 for α corresponds to a standard Poisson model, whereasα>0indicates unequal variance, or excess clustering, in FM across cities, after accounting for the size of each city's population of firm-years at risk. For a similar application of this methodology, see Ellison and Swanson (2016), who estimate negative binomial models to measure cross-school heterogeneity among high-achieving mathematics students.
The first column of TableIIreports the results. The estimate on the number of firm-years is highly significant, with a coefficient close to 1, as expected. More interesting, however, is the finding that the estimated variance parameter (α=0.0628) is highly significant (p<0.001), which strongly rejects the null of equal distributions across cities. To interpret the magnitude, the result indicates that, for a city one standard deviation above the mean, FM is expected to occur about0.0628≈25%more frequently than average.
In the second column, we repeat this exercise but include only FM events for firms with at least $750 million in total assets, the same size cutoff applied in Dyck, Morse, and Zingales's (2010) study of whistle-blowers in corporate fraud cases. Although we dig deeper into enforcement issues later in the paper (SectionV.A), this preliminary filter provides a rough control, to the extent that large and small firms attract a different level of scrutiny from the SEC or other authorities. Despite losing a substantial number of FM cases, the estimated variance parameter not only remains significant (p<0.001), but is even larger in magnitude. Among FM cases involving larger firms, a city one standard deviation above the average is associated with an increase of about0.0913≈30%. Thus, even among firms with fairly similar detection probabilities, there remain significant regional differences in FM.
The preceding tests have two drawbacks. First, they have low statistical power, as each city is allowed to contribute a single degree of freedom to the estimation. Second, city-level heterogeneity is modeled under a specific functional form; if the true distribution ofuiis not gamma, the regression is misspecified. Accordingly, in this section we estimate panel models at the city-year level and model regional heterogeneity using city fixed effects, which produces estimates of city-level heterogeneity unconstrained by a specific a priori distribution.
Panel A of TableIIIreports results from estimating a series of Poisson models. Note the increased number of observations (N=800), which comes from multiplying the number of cities (20) by the number of years in the sample (40). Both right- and left-hand side variables are constructed as before, except that they are now calculated within city-year pairs. So, within a given city-year, they-variable is the number of firms committing FM, with thex-variable including the number of firms at risk and potentially a set of fixed effects. (The variable labels in TableIIIare adjusted to make this explicit.)
Column (1) reports the results including the number of firms at risk in each city-year and year fixed effects. Both are significant at better than the 1% level. Column (2) adds area fixed effects. Although the pseudo-R2increases by only about 2%, city dummies are highly significant (p<0.001).
We also estimate city fixed effects using OLS. Here, we take as the dependent variable the rate of FM observed in each city-year, that is, the number of firms that commit FM divided by the total number of firms. The mean (standard deviation) of this variable over all city-year observations is 1.12% (1.40%). FM rates exceeding 3.04% are observed 10% of the time.
As a benchmark, column (3) of TableIIIreports OLS estimates with only year fixed effects. When city fixed effects are added in column (4), theR2increases by seven percentage points to 0.41. TheF-statistic (p-value) for the joint significance of the city dummies is 4.50 (<0.001). The interquartile range of the estimated city effects is 0.56%, about half the average FM rate across all cities.
article.IV. A Culture of Misconduct:
In the remainder of the paper, we take the above patterns as given and attempt to better understand why they exist. We first consider the possibility that a city's social/cultural norms may influence FM. In SectionIV.A, we attempt to capture city-level culture using observable attributes such as religiosity, education, age, and measures of local economic growth. Though these attributes have some explanatory power, excess city-level clustering in FM remains, which indicates that social norms may be poorly measured by observables. In SectionIV.B, we explore proxies for a city's general attitudes toward right and wrong using measures of behaviors other than FM, such as spousal infidelity or political corruption. We consider a number of extensions and robustness exercises in SectionIV.C, and discuss some interpretational limitations in SectionIV.D.
In this section, we attempt to link FM with attributes that may serve as indicators for a city's ethical norms. These variables, which are informed by prior work, measure the following:
An extensive literature studies the relation between economic growth, usually measured at the country level (though see Glaeser and Saks (2006) and Johnson, LaFountain, and Yamarik (2011)), and various measures of corruption.18While debate remains with respect to both the basic findings and the direction of causation, it is possible that executives' decisions to engage in FM may be influenced by an area's economic health. To account for this possibility, we include as predictors of a city's average FM average population growth, wage growth, and log of income.
Drawing on work suggesting that a person's morality can change as they age (Kohlberg (1984)), we next include as a covariate the (log) average age of residents in a city.
Acknowledging again the difficulties with assigning causation, prior authors document a robust association between education and crime (e.g., Lochner and Moretti (2004)) and/or corruption (e.g., Cheung and Chan (2008)). Thus, to account for the possibility that differences in educational attainment capture, in part, the cultural norms of a city, we include the average number of years of education of residents in a city.
Studies in the accounting and finance literature suggest an association between religious participation and financial fraud. Examples include Dyreng, Mayew, and Williams (2012), who link religious adherence to abnormal accruals (e.g., aggressive revenue recognition), and McGuire, Omer, and Sharp (2012), who examine misreporting as well as class-action lawsuits brought by shareholders. Grullon, Kanatas, and Weston (2009) further include among their outcome variables abnormal CEO compensation. To capture the importance of regional differences in religious participation, we measure a city's average religious participation using the number of religious adherents (from ARDA data) divided by the city's total population.
Column 1 of TableIVreports the results when we attempt to explain cross-city differences in FM using the six variables above, in addition to the number of firm-years for each city. We find that, as a group, these variables do have some explanatory power, as they are jointly significant at the 1% level. The most significant factor is population growth (t=2.14), which indicates that FM is more common in growing cities. Although none of the other variables are individually significant, many of the point estimates have intuitive signs. For example, the coefficient on religious participation is negative, which provides support (albeit weak) for prior work. FM appears to be somewhat less common in wealthy cities, consistent with prior evidence on corruption and economic growth.
Another important finding is that, although these demographic and institutional features appear to matter in aggregate, α remains highly significant (p<0.001), which suggests that substantial city-level clustering in FM remains. Thus, although (measurable) city-level attributes partly explain regional cross-section of FM, they do not provide a complete account of such differences.
An alternative way of measuring the norms of a city is by observing theactionsof its residents. We collect local data on five non-FM misbehaviors, and then test for associations with FM. The first two run afoul of statutes/laws, and thus are potentially subject to enforcement, whereas the latter three violate social (not legal) norms, making detection/enforcement less relevant.
The first type of non-FM misbehavior we consider relates to the corruption-related activities of a city's elected officials. The types of activities prosecuted include electoral fraud, conflicts of interest, campaign violations, and obstructions of justice. Because such activities are illegal, one concern is that regional differences in enforcement may contribute, at least partly, to any observed geographical differences. However, as Glaeser and Saks (2006) discuss, a significant advantage of using political corruption is that it is enforced at the federal level by the DOJ. The federal judicial system's relative isolation from local corruption “should treat people similarly across space (p. 1054).” Any remaining concerns should thus be limited to complementarities between local and nonlocal authorities, such as a local newspaper or district attorney providing the initial lead on a corrupt politician, which federal authorities use in their own detection and enforcement efforts.
Column (2) of TableIVreports the results using a city's average per capita rate of political convictions to predict FM occurrences. The effect is statistically significant (t=1.99), with an estimated semielasticity of 0.0573. Holding all else equal, this implies that, compared to Seattle (1.4 political convictions per 1,000,000 people), Miami (5.4 per 1,000,000) would be expected to have0.057×4≈23%more cases of FM.
Column (3) tests for a relation between regional patterns in misconduct committed by public companies in an area to those committed by the area's registered financial advisors. As with political corruption, we observe a positive association with FM, with an estimated coefficient of 0.0044 (t=1.89;p=8%). Moving from the city with the fifth lowest ranking of financial advisor misconduct (Boston; 48.19 cases per million people) to the city with the fifth highest (San Francisco; 92.11 per million people) would lead to0.0044×43.92≈19%more cases of FM.
The next three measures of non-FM misbehavior are not explicitly illegal and thus capture violations of social contracts rather than legal statutes.
The backdating of stock option grants to lower the exercise price is not per se illegal. Firms generally have complete discretion over the setting of stock option exercise prices. They can use any exercise price-setting methodology they wish, including ones—such as hindsight backdating—that make options in-the-money.
Rather, legal troubles typically arise from the unintended consequences of backdating, such as tax implications arising from having to recalculate earnings over the relevant period.19In any event, given the widespread perception that option backdating represents a way to camouflage executive pay, we take this measure as a questionable, if legal, practice. Thus, variation in backdating likely provides insights into the ethical norms of a city's upper-level corporate management.
In column (4) of TableIVwe include as a covariate each city's rate of option backdating in a predictive model of FM. The estimated coefficient is positive and statistically significant (t=2.07), and indicates that a 6% increase in the percentage of backdating firms—for comparison, the interquartile range for backdating is 1.49% to 7.31%—is associated with a 18% increase in firm-years with FM.
We next relate FM of public firms to the city-level percentage of active Internet users over the age of 18 with an Ashley Madison account. Consistent with its slogan, “Life is short—have an affair,” Ashley Madison explicitly targets married people seeking extramarital affairs. As with backdating and physician rent-seeking (discussed next), spousal infidelity is not explicitly illegal and thus is not subject to concerns related to differential enforcement across cities.
Column (5) reports the results. The coefficient estimate is 1.305, with at-statistic of 2.31. Thus, a one-standard-deviation increase in Ashley Madison usage (0.11) implies a1.305×0.11≈14%increase in public firm FM. This is a little more than half of the difference between the cities at the 25th(Atlanta) and 75th(New York) percentiles.
In our final test, we measure the average sensitivity of a region's physicians to pharmaceutical payments when making their prescribing decisions. Although such practices have raised ethical questions, currently no federal, state, or local statutes forbid such financial relationships (sometimes called “detailing”) or regulate physicians' medical decisions potentially influenced by them.20Thus, as with backdating and Ashley Madison usage, we do not expect regional differences in rent-seeking among physicians to reflect differential enforcement.
The Appendix details our methodology. Broadly speaking, the city-level estimates in TableIindicate how many additional prescriptions a drug company can expect when it has a financial relationship with a given prescriber (e.g., paying for meals and/or speaking fees), after controlling for a number of firm, doctor, and regional factors. Higher numbers indicate a steeper slope between detailing dollars and prescriptions. The results imply that providers in areas such as Miami and Los Angeles are most responsive to payments by drug firms.
Column (6) of TableIVrelates these cross-city differences in prescribing behavior to FM by public firms. The coefficient estimate is positive and statistically significant (t=2.01). Calculating the estimated difference between the city at the 25thpercentile of prescription-payment sensitivity (Phoenix at 8.4) and that at the 75thpercentile (St. Louis at 19.1), we would expect an increase in FM of approximately0.0175×10.7≈19%.
Columns (2) through (6) of TableIVindicate that, individually, various measures of local non-FM misbehavior appear to be cross-sectionally related to misconduct by local executives. Here, we attempt to sharpen the analysis by forming a “misbehavior index” that combines the five types of misbehavior into a single measure.
We first rank each local misbehavior on a scale from 1 to 19 (with Orlando excluded since it cannot be ranked for political corruption) and then average the ranks for each city. Figure2plots a city's average ranking for local misbehaviors (x-axis) against its FM rate (y-axis). The strength of the relation is visually apparent (ρ=0.60), with the best fit line through the 19 data points having a positive slope that is significant at the 0.1% level (t=3.05).
In column (7) of TableIV, we report the results when this index is included as a covariate in the negative binomial regression of FM. Compared to the five individual types of misbehavior, the coefficient on the index is much stronger, with at-statistic of 4.25, which is of particular note given the small sample size. To interpret the magnitude, we compare predicted FM between cities with the lowest average misbehavior rates (Detroit and Minneapolis, ranked #3 and #4 on average) and those with the highest misbehavior rates (New York and Miami, ranked #15 and #16). The coefficient estimate indicates that the number of firm-years of FM should be about12×0.069≈83%higher in New York and Miami, which is fairly close to the difference in actual FM rates shown in TableI.21
Similar results are obtained when we use principal components analysis to construct the index. In column (8), we extract the first principal component of the five types of misbehavior and include this as a covariate in the model. As with the average ranking, the first component is highly significant, with a nearly identicalt-statistic (4.23) compared to the average ranking.
In addition to being highly significant, note the impact of including either misbehavior index (i.e., in columns (7) and (8)) onα̂, the parameter capturing excess clustering of FM between cities. While all five types of misbehavior individually (columns (2) through (6)) reduce the magnitude ofα̂by about one-third relative to the economic and demographic controls, combining them into a composite index renders it statistically insignificant. With either index in columns (7) and (8), the estimated magnitude ofα̂is very close to 0, with ap-value of 0.5. Thus, after accounting for culture using the five types of non-FM misbehavior, any remaining city-level variation can be attributed to sampling error.
In addition to the specifications above, we experiment with alternative ways of constructing the city-level misbehavior index. We tabulate the results in Appendix TablesA.ItoA.IV. TableA.Iuses tercile (column (1)), quartile (column (2)), and quintile (column (3)) rankings rather than the simple sum of ranks. Significant effects are observed in each case. The next three Appendix tables report the results when the misbehavior index is formed using every possible two-way (TableA.II), three-way (TableA.III), and four-way (TableA.IV) combination. Positive coefficients are observed in each case, with 20 of 25 statistically significant at conventional levels.
Recall that, in TableIV, we fail to find much support for city-level variables such as population growth, wealth, and education. One possible reason is that a purely cross-sectional analysis is insufficiently powered (though the same argument applies to the misbehavior index). To explore this issue, Appendix TablesA.VandA.VIpresent results of estimations conducted at the city-year level (similar to TableIII), rather than considering each city as a single observation (TableIV). Although this allows dynamic variables such as population growth and religious participation to vary over time, the results are virtually unchanged. Whether we consider the full sample of firms (TableA.V) or focus on the large-firm subsample (TableA.VI), both first principle component (columns (1)) and average ranking (columns (2)) are significantly related to FM, whereas other city-level variables generally fail to give a reliable relation.
A final concern relates to the time periods for which data on FM and the various types of city-level misbehavior are available. While the KKLM data cover the four decades beginning in 1970, only the political misconduct data go back this far. None of the other types of non-FM misbehavior span even half the sample period—financial advisor misconduct has the best coverage of 15 years ending in 2014—and two, the data on doctors and unfaithful spouses, are recent and, because of their short time dimension, are essentially cross-sectional snapshots.
The empirical relevance of this temporal disconnect depends on the stability of city-level social norms over time. At one extreme, if norms are highly persistent, then there is little problem estimating cross-sectional models such as TableIVusing only a portion of the data to construct the right-hand-side variables. At the other extreme, suppose that each year cities experience i.i.d. shocks that temporarily create dispersion in norms. In this case, there would be no long-lasting (average) differences, which would imply that the observed cross-sectional results in TableIVwould be mostly spurious. An intermediate case would allow for both persistence and dynamics, in the same way that Boston is much colder on average than Miami but may experience hot days from time to time, even hotter than Miami.
The cross-sectional findings in TableIVimplicitly rely on city-level norms being at least partly persistent, which we believe to be reasonable for a number of reasons. First, despite measurement periods that differ (in some cases by decades), cities that score poorly one type of misbehavior tend to score poorly on others. The average correlation between the five index components is about 20%, and is positive for 8 of the 10 possible pairs. Second, many factors that create or sustain a city's norms, in particular, those related to ethnicity or country of origin, move slowly. Indeed, Fisman and Miguel's (2007) study of parking violations among United Nations diplomats finds little evidence of “convergence to U.S. corruption norms (p. 1023),” even for those who left their home countries several years prior.
In an attempt to empirically address the potential mismatch between when a city's FM and other types of misbehavior are measured, we conduct two sets of tests. First, we reestimate the model from the last two columns of TableIVover the most recent half (columns (1) and (2)) and quarter (columns (3) and (4)) of the sample, periods that more closely correspond to the measurement of non-FM types of misbehavior. The results are presented in TableV. In each case, the resultsstrengthenas the sample period becomes more recent, with the last decade showing the strongest effects of a city's culture on FM. Together with the full-sample results in TableIV, these results indicate that, although city-level norms seem to exhibit substantial persistence, dynamics also play a role.
Our second test reduces further the time between when FM and the explanatory (misbehavior) variables are measured. Although we lack a long time series for most of the types of misbehavior, political corruption is an exception, as it is available for all but one city (Orlando, FL) over our full four-decade sample period. Consequently, in this case we can ask whether temporal spikes in a city's political crimes coincide with spikes in white collar crime.
TableVIpresents results of Poisson regressions in which the dependent variable is the count of FM occurrences within each city-year pair. In general, we find some evidence of local co-movement in political corruption and FM, although the demands of this specification weaken the statistical significance. Odd-numbered columns include only year fixed effects, and even-numbered columns include both year and city fixed effects. Moving rightward across the table, lagged (columns (3) to (6)) political corruption appears to be a better predictor of a city's FM than contemporary measures (columns (1) and (2)). Averaging political corruption over multiple years (columns (7) and (8)) gives the strongest results. While the modest significance tempers our conclusions, one possible interpretation of the lead-lag effect is that local politicians are particularly influential in setting social norms, perhaps via their visibility or prominence, as modeled in Acemoglu and Jackson (2015).
We interpret the above as indicating that social norms differ between cities and influence a wide range of decisions and behaviors, including those by local executives. In this section we more rigorously identify what social norms represent and discuss some limitations in interpreting the results.
A traditional tension in sociology features two basic hypotheses for understanding human behavior. One view, which goes back to at least Adam Smith (1776) (but see also Becker (1976)), emphasizes the rational weighing of various tradeoffs. Thishomo economicusparadigm can be contrasted withhomo sociologicus, the latter of which is governed by social norms, which Elster (1989) describes as a collection of “prescribed behavior[s], even if better options become available,” even to the point of rendering the actor a “mindless plaything of social forces” (p. 99). Elster goes on explaining that whereas individual rationality is outcome-oriented, norms are not, but rather derive their power from the desire for other people's approval.22
Norms can also arise in purely rational settings, where the interactions of individuals give rise to multiple equilibria. For example, Sugden (1989) show that norms can serve as a coordination mechanism in such settings, but importantly, they do not always lead to efficient outcomes.23Rather, the key feature of norms is their ability to persist over time, which Sugden (1989) describes in the context of the equilibrium refinementevolutionary stability(Smith and Price (1973)) and defines as “a pattern of behavior such that, if it is generally followed in the population, any small number of people who deviate from it will do less well than the others,” the result ultimately being “self-enforcing” (p. 91). Sugden's (1989) observations about stability are useful for understanding how social norms can vary—potentially for long periods of time—across cities, and are supported by work in other areas indicating geographic heterogeneity in various dimensions, for example, crime (Glaeser, Sacerdote, and Scheinkman (1996)), voting patterns (Ferreira and Gyourko (2009)), and even diet (Li, Zhang, and Pagan (2016)).
That norms appear to differ between cities is a different question fromwhythey do orwhatfactors sustain these differences. A good framework for thinking about this issue is Manski's (1993) description of thereflection problem. Broadly speaking, behavior can differ between groups (cities in our context) because of differences in (1) inherent (exogenous) attributes, such as genetics, (2) environmental (correlated) factors such as enforcement or local economic conditions, and (3) peer (endogenous) effects, where the behavior of some people influences the behavior of others.
Unfortunately, our findings shed little light on these distinctions. For example, our two institutional variables—education and religious participation—have fairly straightforward interpretations as exerting (partly) correlated influences on potentially misbehaving executives, at least over the horizon we examine.24But neither shows a robust effect across specifications. Likewise, if age had a stronger effect on FM, we could be confident that this represents an exogenous effect whereby a city's norms are influenced in part by an aging population (Kohlberg (1984)). However, as with education and religion, it is not a reliable predictor of FM.
Rather, the covariate that works best—the local misbehavior index—is by construction just as endogenous as the variable it seeks to explain (FM). The advantage here is that, because of the wide variety of activities and people involved, it is difficult (at least for us) to imagine some factor other than city-specific norms being responsible for the strong relation between FM and the misbehavior index. Thus, insofar as the goal is to distinguish norms from other potential reasons why FM might vary regionally (e.g., enforcement), this result is helpful.
On the minus side, however, differences in all manner of misbehavior (including FM) could be influenced by any branch of the reflection tree or any combination of branches. For example, genetics (Loewen et al. (2013)) and/or long-standing ancestral factors (Liu (2016)) could be reflected in regional patterns of FM, along with any other types of misbehavior in the index. Likewise, although we do not find much support for educational or religious effects, our measures of correlated influences are both imperfect and incomplete. Better measurement could better identify which environmental factors drive an area's attitudes toward right and wrong. Finally, endogenous peer effects may be important: if violators of rules are more likely to face social stigma (either implicit or explicit) in some areas, we might expect to find regional clustering of misbehavior across a range of activities.
As Leary and Roberts (2014) discuss in the context of capital structure, identifying the marginal effects of peer-to-peer interactions requires a shock that is orthogonal to both correlated and exogenous influences. Clearly, cross-sectional relationships such as those presented in TableIVfail this criteria, and thus cannot credibly distinguish endogenous effects from other potential determinants of local norms. We therefore interpret the observed patterns as evidence that local norms are important for determining a wide range of behaviors, but we remain mostly agnostic about their determinants.
article.V. Alternative Explanations:
In this section we consider the two factors other than social norms identified by the model in SectionI. The first is regional differences in enforcement. If such differences are meaningful, they potentially introduce two empirical challenges. The first is a type of measurement error: even if corporate managers commit FM at the same rate in every city, differential detection could result in higher observed FM in some areas compared to others. The second type of problem is deterrence: if authorities are (at least perceived to be) tougher in some regions than others, managers may be less inclined to take on the risk that FM entails. Although these effects work against rather than reinforce one another, net differences across cities may still exist.
Before conducting a more formal analysis of regional enforcement, it is useful to consider which parties typically expose and/or enforce FM. This allows us to intuitively assess whether these parties are likely to concentrate their efforts disproportionately in certain geographic regions.
The most relevant study for addressing this question is Dyck, Morse, and Zingales's (2010) careful tracking of 216 cases of corporate fraud, which catalogues how and when these cases were exposed. Their headline finding is that no single entity plays a dominant role—industry regulators, law firms, equity holders, the media, and industry competitors all bring FM to light, with no type of party being responsible for more than 20% of detections. Perhaps their most surprising result is the relatively minor role played by the SEC, which blew the whistle on only 10 cases in their sample, or about 7%.
For our purposes, the important question here is whether whistle-blowers concentrate their forensic efforts in specific cities. This may be plausible if, for example, institutional investors tend to hold stocks of local companies, in which case they may more carefully scrutinize local management. If local investors in some cities happen to be more vigilant than in other cities, we might see differences in the detection of misconduct. Similarly, industry regulators may be stricter in some cities than in others, especially around industrial clusters such as Houston (energy) or Detroit (automobiles). Finally, although the SEC appears to play a relatively minor role as a whistle-blower, a “tough” officer rotating to a local office may increase the chance that FM is detected and/or prosecuted, as Kedia and Rajgopal (2011) show.
For most of the remaining players identified by Dyck, Morse, and Zingales (2010), however, it is harder—though not impossible—to imagine geographic motives being of first-order importance. The financial media, for example, exposed almost one-sixth of the cases of fraud in their sample, but national (vs. local) outlets were more likely to be involved. Likewise, whistle-blowers in a firm's supply chain—clients, competitors, and even its own employees—would appear to be motivated to expose fraud in a particular company, not in a geographic area. Nonetheless, to the extent that regional differences in enforcement do exist, the analyses in the next two sections address this more explicitly.
While the activities of most whistle-blowers are not directly observable, the SEC is an exception. Kedia and Rajgopal (2011) use the location of SEC regional offices as a proxy for enforcement strength. In a sample of 331 income-decreasing restatements from 1997 to 2002, they find markedly lower rates for firms headquartered in or around SEC offices, which are located in Washington, DC; New York City; Miami; Chicago; Denver; and Los Angeles.25However, they also find that SEC prosecution rates arehigherfor firms in these six cities—as enforcement costs are an increasing function of distance to the target firm—both as a percentage of all firms (their TableVI, columns (1) and (2)) and as a percentage of restatements (their TableVI, columns (3) and (4)).
These findings suggest a potential role for SEC location as a determinant of our main findings. Accordingly, in column (1) of TableVIIwe add to the negative binomial regression an indicator for an SEC regional office using Kedia and Rajgopal's (2011) classification. Although the statistical significance is marginal (t≈1.7in columns (1) and (2)), which is perhaps not surprising given a cross-sectional regression that includes only 20 observations, the coefficient estimate is positive and economically large (14% semielasticity), which provides some support for Kedia and Rajgopal's findings. However, because the estimated coefficients on the misbehavior indices are minimally affected by including the SEC dummy, this exercise suggests that, even after controlling for differential governmental scrutiny or resources between cities, the effect of cultural norms persists.
A second way to proxy for the strength of enforcement is to use firm size as a filter. Following Dyck, Morse, and Zingales (2010), who argue that focusing on relatively large firms holds detection incentives relatively constant, we consider FM for firms with greater than $750 million in total assets. (Recall that the application of this filter for our benchmark results is reported in column (2) of TableII.) Columns (3) and (4) of TableVIIreport the results.
First, note that the SEC indicator is now far from significant in each specification (t≈0.4), suggesting that firm size is a good control for enforcement strength. Moreover, despite penalizing an already underpowered test with even fewer instances of FM, the estimated coefficient on the misbehavior index not only remains highly significant, but is also substantially larger (by about 80%) than in the full sample. Thus, even after simultaneously controlling for enforcement incentives in two ways—through SEC location and firm size—columns (3) and (4) suggest that regional norms influence executives' behavior even among large companies with less localized operations. Also as before, the estimatedα̂remains insignificant in both columns (p=0.32in column (3) andp=0.22in column (4)), suggesting that, conditional on the covariates (particularly the index), we cannot reject the null that FM occurrences are Poisson distributed with an equal mean (and variance) between cities.26
In addition to differences in enforcement, the model in SectionIidentifies a third type of regional heterogeneity that may generate cross-sectional differences in FM rates, namely, firm heterogeneity. Specifically, firms might differ geographically in the benefits derived from FM, due perhaps to differences in financial distress or executive compensation.
That these, or other firm characteristics, could vary regionally is plausible, given that geographical patterns have been documented in stock returns (Pirinsky and Wang (2006)), investment rates (Dougal, Parsons, and Titman (2015)), leverage and cash balances (Almazan et al. (2010)), and other variables related to firm performance (Cooper and Ovtchinnikov (2015)).
TableVIIIreports results of negative binomial regressions that control for the city-level averages of each of the above attributes. In general, the explanatory power of these attributes is weak. The coefficients on four of the five characteristics havet-statistics with an absolute value less than 1, with only city-level average leverage (t=1.89) showing any relation to misconduct. The next two columns include the first principal component as an explanatory variable, but this too gives a nonresult, regardless of whether the city-level demographic and economic variables discussed in SectionIV.Aare included as controls.
For comparison, the last column of TableVIIIadds the first principal component of the city-level misbehavior. As in prior tables, it is highly significant (t=5.17), and when included in the estimation, accounts for essentially all of the cross-city heterogeneity, as indicated by thep-value on α. Overall, the results in TableVIIIcontinue to support the importance of city-level cultural norms, while providing little evidence that geographical variation in FM has any relationship to firm-level heterogeneity.
Thus far, we estimate models at either the city or city-year level. Here, we conclude by disaggregating the data to the firm-year level, with the dependent variable taking a value of 1 for years involving FM (the violation period), and 0 otherwise. This empirical setup has two advantages. First, it allows us to controldynamicallyfor various firm factors that may proxy for a manager's benefit from engaging in FM, which complements the cross-sectional (time-series average) controls included in TableVIII. Second, it allows us to ask whether, in addition to having different average rates of FM, cities experience localized “outbreaks,” whereby the probability of one firm engaging in FM is a function of the contemporaneous FM rates of its peers.
The results are shown in TableIX. As a benchmark, column (1) reports the results of estimating equation3without firm-level characteristics. Our estimate of β1is 9.83, with at-statistic of 4.83, which indicates that a 1% increase in the contemporaneous FM rates of a firm's local, nonindustry peers increases the odds ratio of it committing FM by aboute.0983−1≈10.3%.27For comparison, we next show the FM sensitivity to a firm's industry peers. With an estimated coefficient of about 13.79 (t=4.62), the industry effect is larger, though not dramatically, than the area effect. Because we also control for both the market's average FM rate and each city's average FM rate over the whole sample, the estimates of β1and β2are indicative of “waves” of FM at both the industry and the city level.
Column (2) adds firm characteristics, most of which have intuitive coefficients. FM is more common among large firms (as the payoff is larger from investigating) and growth firms (which have more incentives to manipulate earnings because they tend to raise more capital). Stock returns are high prior to FM investigations, but leverage is not significant. Thus, rather than distressed firms being more likely to engage in FM, a better interpretation may be that fraudulent accounting is (at least temporarily) effective in fooling the market. Yet, as we see in the first row, β1is minimally affected, with a similar point estimate and statistical significance. This suggests that even time-varying heterogeneity in firm attributes or performance has at best a modest effect on the temporal clustering of FM within cities.
Columns (3) and (4) add to the firm-level controls the percentage of noncash compensation paid to the firm's CEO. Because data on CEO pay are available for only a minority of firms (specifically those covered by ExecuComp), column (3) reestimates the same model as in column (1), but on the smaller sample. Reducing the number of observations by over 80% has a considerable impact on area (β1) and industry (β2) sensitivities, but both remain significant. Paralleling the results in the first two columns, including the full set of firm-level control variables (column (4)), has virtually no impact on the estimated sensitivities.
article.VI. Conclusion:
The evidence in this paper reveals large cross-sectional differences in the average rate of detected financial misconduct among the 20 largest U.S. cities. Indeed, the average rates in high-misconduct cities such as Miami and Dallas tend to be about three times those of low-misconduct cities such as Minneapolis and Indianapolis. Both negative binomial and OLS estimates indicate that the overall dispersion across cities is highly significant.
We explore three sets of explanations for these city-level differences: (1) cultural/social norms, (2) regional disparities in enforcement, and (3) heterogeneity in firm characteristics that determine the marginal benefit of FM. Using an index of five types of non-FM misbehavior from a wide cross-section of society, we document a strong correlation between FM and these proxies for a city's general attitudes toward right and wrong. Importantly, this city-level misbehavior index drives out (statistically significant) clustering in FM across cities, suggesting that it provides a near-complete account for the factors underlying the heterogeneity we document. In contrast, we find little evidence that regional differences in enforcement or firm attributes contribute to FM clustering across cities.
Where our analysis cannot be as specific is with respect to the determinants of an area's social norms. Ancestral heterogeneity may play some role, though it is worthy noting that our sample includes only large, and in most cases relatively diverse, American cities. Likewise, common environmental factors may play a role in creating or propagating ethical norms, with potential contributions from religious organizations, the educational system, the media, or civic organizations. Although our results in this context are not promising, the set of correlated factors we examine is limited. Accordingly, we interpret our findings as providing support for the existence and importance of city-level norms, but remain agnostic about their ultimate cause(s). We view this as a fruitful area for future research.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
article.Appendix A: Calculation of Prescription-:
To estimate these differences across cities, we collect data on payments to physicians provided on the Dollars for Docs website, a searchable web database that allows a user to observe transfers from pharmaceutical companies to specific physicians. This database is hosted by independent journalist consortium ProPublica.28In 2010, 11 pharmaceutical firms reported payments on Dollars for Docs, including most major drug firms such as Pfizer, Merck, Glaxo, Astrazeneca, and Johnson and Johnson. Payments are broken down by both dollar amount and type, for instance, gifts, meals, speaking, travel, consulting, and, on occasion, proprietary research. We merge this data set with Medicare (Part D) prescription information for each doctor, also in 2010. Together, these data sources allow us to test for an association between pecuniary transfers and the 129,594 registered physicians practicing in economic areas corresponding to the 20 largest U.S. cities.
The unit of observation is at the doctor-company level, and thus the dependent variable is the number of prescriptions written by a given doctor for drugs manufactured by a given company. We are interested in the coefficient β1, which tells us, on the margin, whether payments between a given doctor-firm pair are associated with more prescriptions. In addition toPayment, we also control for the number of generic prescriptions written by each doctor to account for differences in general prescription intensity. Also included are dummy variables for 416 precisely defined medical specialties, such as pediatric oncology or oculoplastic surgery.
We parametrizePaymentdoc,firmwith a dummy variable that takes a value of 1 if any payment is observed for that doctor-firm pair, and 0 otherwise.29TableIindicates that, on average, the presence of a payment—the median of which is a mere $61—is associated with about 14 additional prescriptions for the paying firm's drugs. However, more interesting is the variation across cities, with Miami's doctors being almost three times as sensitive (39 prescriptions) compared to the average, and Minneapolis's physicians much less so (5). Further, as we saw with political corruption, cities ranking high in prescription-payment sensitivity also rank high in FM rates, for example, Miami has both the highest FM rate as well as the highest prescription-payment sensitivity (and the second highest political corruption rate).
Before proceeding, it is worth mentioning that there are numerous reasons to expect a positive relation between payments and prescriptions. Possible mechanisms include reverse causality (i.e., where doctors already prescribing a company's drugs are rewarded with payments), information being transferred to doctors from meetings with pharmaceutical representatives, and simple rent-seeking and/or corruption, whereby the payments themselves provide incentives for doctors to tilt prescriptions in the way observed. Although a detailed discussion of the various mechanisms is beyond the scope of this paper (see Engelberg, Parsons, and Tefft (2016)), note that the correlations observed in TableIprovide prima facie evidence for a rent-seeking explanation. It would be a striking coincidence if doctors in Miami, where political corruption and FM rates are very high, happened to find educational offerings by pharmaceutical representatives more persuasive than those in Minneapolis, where corruption of any type is relatively unusual.
